# Conclusion

Our recommendation is to incorporate pre-lecture videos into the COMP110 curriculum, as students regard them as a potential method to increase understanding and better manage the perceived difficulty of the course. Overall, our data analysis supports this idea.

First, the categorical bar chart shows that a clear majority of students (73.5%) expressed interest in the implementation of pre-lecture videos, indicating strong overall demand. Additionally, the boxplot comparing perceived difficulty with interest in pre-lecture videos suggests that students who find the course more difficult tend to express greater interest in these resources. This relationship is further supported by the line graph, where the average perceived difficulty increases alongside interest in pre-lecture videos. Together, these findings suggest that students who struggle more with the course are more likely to seek additional support such as pre-lecture videos.

A similar pattern appears when considering students’ self-reported understanding. The boxplot indicates that students who perceive themselves as having lower levels of understanding tend to show higher interest in pre-lecture videos. This reinforces the idea that such videos could serve as a helpful preparatory resource, particularly for students who feel less confident about the material.

However, it is important to note that this analysis reflects student demand rather than the actual effectiveness of pre-lecture videos, as they are not currently implemented in the course. While the data suggests that these videos may be beneficial, further evaluation would be needed to measure their direct impact on learning outcomes.

A major potential trade-off is the possible decrease in lecture attendance. Since pre-lecture videos provide an initial introduction to course material, students may feel less incentivized to attend in-person lectures, especially when combined with existing resources such as posted slides and post-lecture quizzes. This could reduce in-class engagement and participation.

In terms of costs and potential impacts on stakeholders, implementing pre-lecture videos would require additional time and effort from instructional staff, including professors and TAs, to design and produce the content. This added workload may affect other aspects of teaching, such as the quality of in-class instruction or the availability of support outside of class. Therefore, while pre-lecture videos show strong potential as a supportive learning tool, their implementation should be carefully balanced with these considerations.
